dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for chest pain.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Chest pain, pressure, sharp feeling. It started about two hours ago as I was lying in bed.
It got worse when I sat up but felt better when I was walking. It's gone now, but my left shoulder still hurts.
Are you alert and oriented?
Yes, I'm aware it's today and this is my house in [CITY].
Can you tell me your full name and where we are?
My name is Michael, and I'm at my house.
That's good. You're awake and oriented to person, place, and time-that is A&Ox4.
I feel fine now, no real distress. I have no headache either.
I'll check your pupils. Can I shine a light in your eyes?
Go ahead.
Your pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light-PERRL-that's great.
And your pupils are about 3mm in size.
Pulse is 128, your breathing is regular and not labored.
Your respirations are 20 per minute. We do see some increased effort, but no accessory muscle use noted.
Blood pressure is 144 over 74.
Oxygen saturation is 99% on room air.
Glucose is not currently available, but we'll keep monitoring.
Your airway is clear and self-maintained without difficulties.
I'll place you on a cardiac monitor to detect any arrhythmias. This is a 4-lead setup for now.
The monitor shows sinus tachycardia. We are going to complete a 12-lead ECG now.
On a scale of 1 to 10, how bad was the chest pain for you?
It was about a 4, but it completely resolved before you came.
You mentioned a history of anxiety. Have you had similar symptoms or chest pain before today?
No, this is the first time. My grandfather had heart issues when he was in his 20's, though.
What medications are you currently taking?
I take Adderall for ADHD and Buspar for anxiety. No known allergies.
You have full range of motion in all your limbs and your gait is steady.
Have you had any shortness of breath, nausea, or headache?
No, I've had no shortness of breath or nausea. I also have no headache.
Your breathing has slowed to 16 per minute now, still normal and not labored.
Your heart rate has improved to 102 since we arrived.
You do not show signs of distress. Breathing is clear with no shortness of breath reported.
Range of motion and mobility remain intact during and after your movement to the stretcher.
You'll be secured with five-point seatbelt restraints for your safety during the ride.
We gave you 324mg of aspirin orally via protocol to help reduce cardiac risk. You said you do not want IV and will wait for it at the ED.
A second 12-lead ECG was taken during transport and again showed no STEMI, only sinus tachycardia.
The second EKG is the same-no heart damage noted. Rhythm remains sinus tach.
Your GCS is still 15-Eyes 4, Verbal 5, Motor 6. No change in mental status or new symptoms during transport.
Your heart rhythm remains stable, and your skin stays warm and dry throughout the ride.
Skin appearance and color remained consistent throughout the ride.
No swelling or cyanosis noted in the limbs at any point.
All evaluations showed no issues with shock or perfusion.
No discoloration was observed in the skin at any point.
Ambulatory patient was securely fastened using five-point harness before transport began.
No distress, no shortness of breath, and nothing alarming observed during entire transportation.
Patient ambulated into the waiting room at receiving facility without assistance after leaving the stretcher.
Contact with [COMPANY_NAME] Emergency Department was initiated during the ride enroute.
Patient was initially found upright and independently transported to the stretcher without difficulty.
During transport, the patient was placed on a RAA stretcher and remained stable.
Patient remained ambulatory and walked with minimal assistance, maintaining stable gait.
No physical assistance was required when moving from the stretcher to the wheelchair at the hospital.

Here's the report: 18-year-old male with chest pain (4/10, pressure and sharp quality, radiated to his left shoulder). Currently no active chest pain. Denies SOB, HA, or nausea. ASA 324mg administered. Vital signs improved with BP at 136/73 and pulse at 102. Skin normal in color and temperature. No accessory muscle use noted. 12-lead ECGs unremarkable for STEMI. Transported to [COMPANY_NAME] System per patient's request.
Patient's friend was secured in a harness and remained in the waiting area.
All restraints were safely released prior to transferring the patient to the ED stretcher.
Report and care were transferred to the receiving ED nurse.
No incident, no complaints, and no resumption of prior symptoms during transport.
We did a repeat 12 lead ECG enroute and found no change from prior findings.
The results still show sinus tach in the young adult male age group.
The patient remained in sinus tach but no acute interventions were needed.
Skin appearance and color remained consistent throughout the ride.
No signs of pallor or discoloration in the patient at any point.
No swelling, cyanosis, or issues with circulation noted in limbs.
All evaluations showed no shock or perfusion concerns.
Airway remained open, and no airway devices were used.
Patient showed coordination when moving with minimal assistance.
Patient was monitored during movements for safety and compliance.
Patient remained conscious and gave no indication of confusion or disorientation.
RAA stretcher was used for direct transport to the selected receiving ED.
Upon arrival, the patient was helped into the wheelchair for comfort and ease.
ED nurse received all report details including recent vitals and medication info.
All documentation, including ECG results, was handed off to the ED nurse.
Patient was coherent and wanted to be with his companion at the ED.
Ridealong passenger was released after arrival and signature on report.
Patient was stable and able to express preferences during the transport phase.
All actions with the patient were done with full consent and no resistance.
No decisions involving airway or fluid replacement made as no issues arose.
No emotional difficulties or behavioral problems were noted during the ride.
Patient was aware of the events and able to coordinate assistance as needed.
All communication done in English with no language support needed.
Transport to [COMPANY_NAME] System took place per patient request.
No issues noted with restraints while in the ambulance during movement.
No falls or sudden health changes reported during the trip.
Patient's skin remained normal with no dryness or excessive sweat.
Pupil size remained unchanged and reactive to light throughout the transport.
Transport padding and supports used in all transfers with standard precautions.
The ambulance moved smoothly without technical or mechanical issues.
Continuous monitoring done every five minutes as per protocol.
No additional doses or treatments were given as per ECG and vital findings.
No exposure concerns or environmental risks were recorded.
Final exit from the ambulance was calm and free of issues.
All individuals, including the ridealong, were accounted for during final checks.
Tasks were divided and completed properly using team roles.
All transported equipment was verified and reset by the team.
No violations or procedures outside of protocol were performed.
Final report was documented and submitted to the hospital as required.
EKG findings were confirmed with the ED crew during the handoff.
All restraints were checked for safety before departure from and arrival to the ED.
Movement was done in a way supportive of patient-stated preferences.
Everything done while on route was properly covered in final documentation.
Patient and friend arrived at ED alive and alert per all EMS transfer protocols.
